records.title: Concepts and Architecture
records.heading: Concepts
records.content: Understand how works under the hood. Read these when you need to understand why, not just how.
records.description: Explanations of internals: workflow execution model, data flow, credential management, and security architecture.

records.title: Intelligent knowledge system architecture
records.heading: Intelligent knowledge system architecture
records.content: The intelligent knowledge system is a structured layer that stores reusable modules, metadata, and intent mappings so humans and AI can retrieve the same trusted product knowledge. python3 scripts/validate_knowledge_modules.py The pipeline keeps authored modules in knowledge_modules/*.yml, validates them, and assembles clean output documents and channel bundles. This preserves normal documentation readability while enabling AI-native retrieval and reuse.
records.description: Learn how the pipeline models reusable knowledge modules for AI retrieval, dynamic assembly, and multi-channel documentation delivery.

records.title: Intelligent knowledge system architecture
records.heading: Core components
records.content: 1. Knowledge modules: atomic YAML units with intent, audience, channel, dependency, and owner metadata. 1. Intent assembler: creates audience-specific docs pages and channel bundles from active modules. 1. Retrieval index: exports module-level records to docs/assets/knowledge-retrieval-index.json. 1. Quality gates: checks schema, dependency integrity, cycle safety, and content completeness.
records.description: Learn how the pipeline models reusable knowledge modules for AI retrieval, dynamic assembly, and multi-channel documentation delivery.

records.title: Intelligent knowledge system architecture
records.heading: Why this improves documentation quality
records.content: Traditional pages duplicate content across docs, in-product guidance, and assistant prompts. Modules let you author once and distribute consistently. - You reduce contradictory guidance because one module powers multiple channels. - You improve AI response quality because retrieval uses intent and audience metadata. - You cut update time because a verified module updates all downstream experiences.
records.description: Learn how the pipeline models reusable knowledge modules for AI retrieval, dynamic assembly, and multi-channel documentation delivery.

records.title: Intelligent knowledge system architecture
records.heading: Data model
records.content: Each module defines: - id, title, summary, and owner - intents, such as configure, secure, or troubleshoot - audiences, such as operator or support - channels, such as docs, assistant, or automation - dependencies for module composition order - content blocks for each channel output
records.description: Learn how the pipeline models reusable knowledge modules for AI retrieval, dynamic assembly, and multi-channel documentation delivery.

records.title: Intelligent knowledge system architecture
records.heading: Operational flow
records.content: 1. Validate modules with npm run lint:knowledge. 1. Assemble docs and bundles with npm run build:intent. 1. Generate retrieval artifacts with npm run build:knowledge-index. 1. Run npm run validate:knowledge as a pre-release gate.
records.description: Learn how the pipeline models reusable knowledge modules for AI retrieval, dynamic assembly, and multi-channel documentation delivery.

records.title: Intelligent knowledge system architecture
records.heading: Security and governance
records.content: Use owner fields and verification dates to enforce accountability. - Assign one owner per module. - Verify security-sensitive modules every 30 days. - Deprecate stale modules by changing status to deprecated.
records.description: Learn how the pipeline models reusable knowledge modules for AI retrieval, dynamic assembly, and multi-channel documentation delivery.

records.title: How the workflow execution model works
records.heading: How the workflow execution model works
records.content: The execution model determines how data flows from one node to the next within a workflow. executes nodes sequentially, passing data as arrays of JSON objects. Understanding this model helps you build reliable workflows and debug unexpected behavior.
records.description: processes workflows by executing nodes sequentially, passing data as arrays of JSON objects between each node in the chain.

hierarchy.lvl2: How the workflow execution model works

records.title: How the workflow execution model works
records.heading: Data structure between nodes
records.content: Every node in receives and outputs data in the same format: an array of items, where each item is a JSON object wrapped in a json key. [  { "json": { "name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com" } },  { "json": { "name": "Bob", "email": "bob@example.com" } } ] When a node receives 5 items, it processes each item independently. The Slack node, for example, sends 5 separate messages—one per item.
records.description: processes workflows by executing nodes sequentially, passing data as arrays of JSON objects between each node in the chain.

records.title: How the workflow execution model works
records.heading: Execution flow
records.content: flowchart LR  A[Trigger] -->|items| B[Node 1]  B -->|transformed items| C[Node 2]  C -->|items| D{IF condition}  D -->|true| E[Branch A]  D -->|false| F[Branch B] follows these rules during execution: 1. The trigger node starts the workflow and produces the initial items. 1. Each subsequent node receives all output items from the previous node. 1. A node processes items either once for all items or once per item, depending on the node type and configuration. 1. Branch nodes (IF, Switch) route items to different paths based on conditions. 1. Execution stops when all branches reach their final nodes.
records.description: processes workflows by executing nodes sequentially, passing data as arrays of JSON objects between each node in the chain.

records.title: How the workflow execution model works
records.heading: Execution modes
records.content: supports two execution modes that affect error handling and performance. === "Regular mode (default)"  Nodes execute one at a time. If a node fails, execution stops and the workflow reports an error. This mode is predictable and easier to debug.  Set in environment: EXECUTIONS_MODE=regular === "Queue mode (production)"  Workflow executions are distributed across worker processes. This mode handles high-volume workloads (hundreds of concurrent executions) and requires a Redis instance for coordination.  Set in environment: EXECUTIONS_MODE=queue
records.description: processes workflows by executing nodes sequentially, passing data as arrays of JSON objects between each node in the chain.

records.title: How the workflow execution model works
records.heading: Error handling
records.content: When a node fails, behavior depends on the node's error handling setting: - Stop execution (default): The workflow stops. The error appears in the execution log. - Continue on fail: The node outputs an error object, and the next node receives it. Use this for non-critical steps like logging. - Error Trigger workflow: starts a separate workflow to handle the error. Use this for alerting and retry logic.
records.description: processes workflows by executing nodes sequentially, passing data as arrays of JSON objects between each node in the chain.

records.title: How the workflow execution model works
records.heading: Key implications for documentation writers
records.content: The execution model means that every node reference page should document what input format the node expects, what output format it produces (how many items, what structure), and how the node behaves with multiple items (once for all vs. once per item).
records.description: processes workflows by executing nodes sequentially, passing data as arrays of JSON objects between each node in the chain.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.content: {{ product_name }} webhook processing pipeline enables real-time event ingestion with HMAC verification, queued processing, and reliable retries. This guide shows you how to deploy a production-ready endpoint in 15 minutes while sustaining up to {{ rate_limit_requests_per_minute }} requests per minute.
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Verify prerequisites and runtime versions
records.content: Before you start, verify access and runtime requirements: - {{ product_name }} {{ current_version }} or later - Access to your {{ product_name }} workspace and deployment settings - A webhook signing key stored in {{ env_vars.encryption_key }} - Public callback URL configured through {{ env_vars.webhook_url }} {{ product_name }} --version node --version python3 --version For endpoint and schema details, see the API reference.
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Configure ingress for cloud and self-hosted
records.content: === "Cloud" \11. Open {{ product_name }} Cloud and create a webhook endpoint. \11. Set callback URL from {{ env_vars.webhook_url }}. \11. Save signing key in your platform secret manager. \11. Enable request throttling at {{ rate_limit_requests_per_minute }} requests per minute. === "Self-hosted" \11. Expose your listener on port {{ default_port }} behind a reverse proxy. \11. Store signing key in {{ env_vars.encryption_key }}. \11. Set the callback URL in {{ env_vars.webhook_url }}. \11. Apply payload limit {{ max_payload_size_mb }} MB at ingress.
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Configure HMAC-SHA256 signature verification
records.content: Use this Python implementation for signature checks and replay protection: import hashlib import hmac import time def verify_webhook_signature(payload_body, signature_header, secret):     """Verify HMAC-SHA256 webhook signature with replay protection."""     try:         parts = dict(item.split("=", 1) for item in signature_header.split(","))         timestamp = int(parts["t"])         received_sig = parts["v1"]     except (ValueError, KeyError):         return False     now = int(time.time())     if abs(now - timestamp) > 300:         return False     signed_payload = f"{timestamp}.{payload_body}".encode("utf-8")     expected_sig = hmac.new(secret.encode("utf-8"), signed_payload, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()     return hmac.compare_digest(expected_sig, received_sig)
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Test verification
records.content: TEST_TIMESTAMP = int(time.time()) test_payload = '{"event": "order.completed", "order_id": "ord_1234", "amount": 2999}' test_secret = "whsec_test_secret_key_abc123" msg = f"{TEST_TIMESTAMP}.{test_payload}".encode("utf-8") test_sig = hmac.new(test_secret.encode("utf-8"), msg, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest() test_header = f"t={TEST_TIMESTAMP},v1={test_sig}" result = verify_webhook_signature(test_payload, test_header, test_secret) print("Signature valid:", result) Use the equivalent Node.js verification in worker services: const crypto = require('crypto'); function verifyWebhookSignature(payload, signatureHeader, secret) {   const parsed = Object.fromEntries(     signatureHeader.split(',').map((part) => part.split('='))   );   const timestamp = Number(parsed.t);   const received = parsed.v1;   if (!Number.isFinite(timestamp)) {     return false;   }   if (Math.abs(Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000) - timestamp) > 300) {     return false;   }   const signedPayload = ${timestamp}.${payload};   const
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Set up async queue processing and retries
records.content: Queue ingestion immediately after validation and return 200 OK without waiting for business processing. Use retry intervals of 1 second, 5 seconds, and 30 seconds with exponential backoff for transient failures. !!! info "Payload size limit"     {{ product_name }} accepts webhook payloads up to {{ max_payload_size_mb }} MB. Reject larger payloads at ingress to protect queue workers. !!! warning "Signature verification required"     Verify each signature before parsing JSON payloads. Do not enqueue events that fail verification. !!! tip "Replay protection"     Require a signed timestamp and reject requests older than five minutes to block replay attacks.
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Verify end-to-end delivery flow
records.content: sequenceDiagram     participant C as Client     participant G as API Gateway     participant V as HMAC Validator     participant Q as Event Queue     participant P as Processor     participant D as Database     C->>G: POST /webhooks (signed payload)     G->>V: Validate HMAC-SHA256     V-->>G: Signature valid     G->>Q: Enqueue event     Q->>P: Process async     P->>D: Store result     P-->>C: 200 OK (acknowledged)
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Track performance and reliability targets
records.content: Use these baseline targets for production rollout: - Throughput: 200 webhooks per second sustained - Verification latency: less than 2 ms median - Queue processing: 1,500 events per second across workers - Retry policy: 1 second, 5 seconds, and 30 seconds exponential backoff - Storage retention: 30 days for event logs and replay analysis
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Set up a real-time webhook processing pipeline
records.heading: Troubleshoot signature, replay, and timeout failures
records.content: \11. Problem: Signature mismatch.    Cause: The service modified payload encoding before verification.    Solution: Use the raw request body bytes, preserve UTF-8 encoding, and verify before JSON parsing. \11. Problem: Replay attack detected.    Cause: Your server clock drift exceeds five-minute tolerance.    Solution: Enable NTP synchronization and keep replay window at 300 seconds. \11. Problem: Connection timeout from webhook provider.    Cause: Synchronous business logic blocks HTTP response.    Solution: Return 200 OK immediately after enqueue, then process asynchronously.
records.description: Configure end-to-end webhook ingestion with HMAC verification, async queue processing, and delivery guarantees in under 15 minutes.

records.title: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes
records.heading: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes
records.content: A workflow is a series of connected nodes that process data automatically. In this tutorial you create a workflow that receives an HTTP request via a Webhook node and sends a notification to Slack.
records.description: Create a webhook-triggered workflow that receives HTTP requests and sends Slack notifications. No coding required.

hierarchy.lvl2: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes

records.title: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.content: - An instance (Cloud or self-hosted). See the getting started overview. - A Slack workspace where you can add apps.
records.description: Create a webhook-triggered workflow that receives HTTP requests and sends Slack notifications. No coding required.

records.title: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes
records.heading: Step 1: Create a new workflow
records.content: === "Cloud" - Log in to your Cloud instance. - Select New Workflow from the top-right menu. - The canvas opens with an empty workflow. === "Self-hosted" - Open your instance at http://localhost:5678. - Select New Workflow. - The canvas opens with an empty workflow.
records.description: Create a webhook-triggered workflow that receives HTTP requests and sends Slack notifications. No coding required.

records.title: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes
records.heading: Step 2: Add a Webhook trigger node
records.content: 1. Select the + button on the canvas. 1. Search for Webhook and select it. 1. Set HTTP Method to POST. 1. Copy the Test URL—you will need it in Step 5. !!! info "Test URL vs Production URL"  The Test URL is active only while the workflow editor is open. The Production URL activates after you toggle the workflow to Active.
records.description: Create a webhook-triggered workflow that receives HTTP requests and sends Slack notifications. No coding required.

hierarchy.lvl2: Step 2: Add a Webhook trigger node

records.title: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes
records.heading: Step 3: Add a Slack node
records.content: 1. Select the + button after the Webhook node. 1. Search for Slack and select it. 1. Set Operation to Send a Message. 1. Select your Slack credential or create one (requires a Slack Bot Token with chat:write scope). 1. Set Channel to your target channel name or ID. 1. Set Text to an expression: New webhook received: {% raw %}{{ $json.body.message }}{% endraw %}
records.description: Create a webhook-triggered workflow that receives HTTP requests and sends Slack notifications. No coding required.

records.title: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes
records.heading: Step 4: Test the workflow
records.content: 1. Select Test Workflow in the top bar. 1. In a terminal, send a test request: curl -X POST YOUR_TEST_URL \  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \  -d '{"message": "Hello from my first workflow!"}' 1. Check your Slack channel—the message appears within 2 seconds.
records.description: Create a webhook-triggered workflow that receives HTTP requests and sends Slack notifications. No coding required.

records.title: Build your first workflow in 5 minutes
records.heading: Step 5: Activate the workflow
records.content: 1. Toggle the workflow to Active in the top-right corner. 1. Replace the Test URL with the Production URL in your application. The workflow now runs automatically for every incoming request, without the editor open.
records.description: Create a webhook-triggered workflow that receives HTTP requests and sends Slack notifications. No coding required.

records.title: Configure webhook triggers
records.heading: Configure webhook triggers
records.content: The {{ product_name }} webhook trigger node starts workflows when it receives HTTP requests. It supports GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE methods with built-in authentication options including Basic Auth, Header Auth, and JWT validation.
records.description: Set up and configure webhook trigger nodes to start workflows from incoming HTTP requests with authentication.

records.title: Configure webhook triggers
records.heading: Before you start
records.content: You need: - {{ product_name }} {{ current_version }} or later - Access to create workflows - A publicly accessible URL (or a tunnel for local development)
records.description: Set up and configure webhook trigger nodes to start workflows from incoming HTTP requests with authentication.

records.title: Configure webhook triggers
records.heading: Create a webhook trigger
records.content: 1. Open the {{ product_name }} editor 1. Add a new workflow 1. Select Webhook as the trigger node 1. Choose the HTTP method (POST is recommended for event data) 1. Copy the generated webhook URL === "Cloud"     {{ product_name }} Cloud provides a public URL automatically:     ``text     {{ cloud_url }}/webhook/your-workflow-id     ` === "Self-hosted"     Configure your instance URL with the {{ env_vars.webhook_url }}     environment variable:     `bash     export {{ env_vars.webhook_url }}="https://your-domain.example.com"     `     Your webhook URL follows this pattern:     `text     https://your-domain.example.com:{{ default_port }}/webhook/your-workflow-id     ``
records.description: Set up and configure webhook trigger nodes to start workflows from incoming HTTP requests with authentication.

records.title: Configure webhook triggers
records.heading: Configure authentication
records.content: !!! warning "Secure your webhooks"     Always enable authentication on production webhook endpoints.     Unauthenticated webhooks accept requests from any source. Add authentication in the webhook node settings: | Auth method | Use case | Configuration | |-------------|----------|---------------| | Header Auth | API key validation | Set header name and expected value | | Basic Auth | Username and password | Set credentials in node settings | | JWT | Token-based auth | Configure secret and algorithm |
records.description: Set up and configure webhook trigger nodes to start workflows from incoming HTTP requests with authentication.

records.title: Configure webhook triggers
records.heading: Test webhook delivery
records.content: Send a test request to verify your webhook works: curl -X POST https://your-domain.example.com:5678/webhook/test \   -H "Content-Type: application/json" \   -d '{"event": "test", "timestamp": "2026-03-07T12:00:00Z"}' Expected response: {   "status": "accepted",   "executionId": "exec_abc123" } !!! tip "Use the test URL first"     {{ product_name }} provides separate test and production webhook     URLs. Use the test URL during development to inspect payloads     without triggering downstream actions.
records.description: Set up and configure webhook trigger nodes to start workflows from incoming HTTP requests with authentication.

records.title: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.heading: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.content: Use this guide when a client asks for Ask AI runtime features such as a live endpoint, an embeddable widget, and billing webhook hooks. npm run askai:runtime:install npm run askai:status
records.description: Install the optional Ask AI runtime pack with API endpoint, widget, auth checks, and billing hooks in a few commands.

records.title: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.heading: Before you start
records.content: You need: - Pipeline repository installed in the client project - config/ask-ai.yml present - Python 3.10 or newer
records.description: Install the optional Ask AI runtime pack with API endpoint, widget, auth checks, and billing hooks in a few commands.

records.title: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.heading: Step 1: Install the runtime pack
records.content: Run: npm run askai:runtime:install This creates ask-ai-runtime/ with: - FastAPI server (app/main.py) - auth guards (app/auth.py) - billing hooks (app/billing_hooks.py) - retrieval helpers (app/retrieval.py) - widget script (public/ask-ai-widget.js) - .env.example and runtime README.md
records.description: Install the optional Ask AI runtime pack with API endpoint, widget, auth checks, and billing hooks in a few commands.

records.title: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.heading: Step 2: Configure Ask AI module
records.content: Enable Ask AI and select billing mode: npm run askai:enable npm run askai:configure -- --provider openai --billing-mode user-subscription --model gpt-4.1-mini
records.description: Install the optional Ask AI runtime pack with API endpoint, widget, auth checks, and billing hooks in a few commands.

records.title: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.heading: Step 3: Configure runtime environment
records.content: cd ask-ai-runtime cp .env.example .env Fill these values in .env: - ASK_AI_API_KEY - ASK_AI_PROVIDER_API_KEY - ASK_AI_WEBHOOK_SECRET
records.description: Install the optional Ask AI runtime pack with API endpoint, widget, auth checks, and billing hooks in a few commands.

records.title: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.heading: Step 4: Start runtime server
records.content: python3 -m venv .venv source .venv/bin/activate pip install -r requirements.txt uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8090 Health check: curl http://localhost:8090/healthz
records.description: Install the optional Ask AI runtime pack with API endpoint, widget, auth checks, and billing hooks in a few commands.

records.title: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.heading: Step 5: Embed the widget
records.content: Add this snippet to the docs site page template or custom HTML block: <script   src="/ask-ai/public/ask-ai-widget.js"   data-ask-ai-endpoint="https://docs.example.com/ask-ai/api/v1/ask"   data-ask-ai-api-key="YOUR_PUBLIC_OR_PROXY_KEY"   data-user-id="USER_123"   data-user-role="support"   data-plan="pro"   data-enabled="true"></script>
records.description: Install the optional Ask AI runtime pack with API endpoint, widget, auth checks, and billing hooks in a few commands.

records.title: Install Ask AI runtime pack
records.heading: Runtime pack install fails because destination exists
records.content: Use force mode: npm run askai:runtime:install:force
records.description: Install the optional Ask AI runtime pack with API endpoint, widget, auth checks, and billing hooks in a few commands.

records.title: Run API-first production flow
records.heading: Run API-first production flow
records.content: This flow turns planning notes into a validated OpenAPI contract, generated stubs, and a testable sandbox with repeatable automation.
records.description: Generate OpenAPI from planning notes, run full lint and self-verification, publish playground assets, and keep a sandbox ready for client testing.

records.title: Run API-first production flow
records.heading: Step 1: Prepare planning notes
records.content: Use this input artifact format: Project: TaskStream API version: v1 Base URL: https://api.taskstream.example.com/v1 Status: Draft for OpenAPI writing Store notes in demos/api-first/taskstream-planning-notes.md.
records.description: Generate OpenAPI from planning notes, run full lint and self-verification, publish playground assets, and keep a sandbox ready for client testing.

records.title: Run API-first production flow
records.heading: Step 2: Run generation and verification
records.content: Run the universal flow command: python3 scripts/run_api_first_flow.py \   --project-slug taskstream \   --notes demos/api-first/taskstream-planning-notes.md \   --spec api/openapi.yaml \   --spec-tree api/taskstream \   --docs-provider mkdocs \   --verify-user-path \   --mock-base-url http://localhost:4010/v1 \   --auto-remediate \   --max-attempts 3 The runner performs: 1. OpenAPI generation from notes. 1. Contract validation. 1. Spectral, Redocly, and Swagger CLI lint checks. 1. FastAPI stub generation. 1. Self-verification of operation coverage and user-path calls.
records.description: Generate OpenAPI from planning notes, run full lint and self-verification, publish playground assets, and keep a sandbox ready for client testing.

records.title: Run API-first production flow
records.heading: Step 3: Start sandbox for live testing
records.content: For contract-mock mode: bash scripts/api_sandbox_project.sh up taskstream ./api/openapi.yaml 4010 For prod-like mode on VPS: bash scripts/api_prodlike_project.sh up taskstream 4011
records.description: Generate OpenAPI from planning notes, run full lint and self-verification, publish playground assets, and keep a sandbox ready for client testing.

hierarchy.lvl2: Step 3: Start sandbox for live testing

records.title: Run API-first production flow
records.heading: Step 4: Keep sandbox always on
records.content: Use Docker restart policy and health checks through: - docker-compose.api-sandbox.prodlike.yml - restart: unless-stopped - built-in healthcheck on /v1/healthz
records.description: Generate OpenAPI from planning notes, run full lint and self-verification, publish playground assets, and keep a sandbox ready for client testing.

records.title: Run API-first production flow
records.heading: Step 5: Stop sandbox when needed
records.content: bash scripts/api_sandbox_project.sh down taskstream ./api/openapi.yaml 4010 bash scripts/api_prodlike_project.sh down taskstream 4011
records.description: Generate OpenAPI from planning notes, run full lint and self-verification, publish playground assets, and keep a sandbox ready for client testing.

records.title: Run API-first production flow
records.heading: Next steps
records.content: - TaskStream API playground - TaskStream API planning notes

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.content: This is the single source of truth for how to sell and run the pipeline today.
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: 1. Core promise
records.content: One-time setup, then smooth weekly automation: \11. Generate/configure client profile. \11. Provision bundle into client repo. \11. Install scheduler. \11. Weekly reports and checks run automatically. \11. Human only reviews report + final docs.
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: 2. One-time setup (you do this)
records.content: \11. Pick preset: - profiles/clients/presets/small.yml - profiles/clients/presets/startup.yml - profiles/clients/presets/enterprise.yml \11. Fastest path: python3 scripts/onboard_client.py \11. If you use manual mode, customize client profile: - client identity - repo paths (docs_root, api_root, sdk_root) - output targets - module toggles - policy/plan strictness \11. Provision (manual mode): python3 scripts/provision_client_repo.py \   --client profiles/clients/<client>.client.yml \   --client-repo /path/to/client-repo \   --docsops-dir docsops \   --install-scheduler linux Windows: python3 scripts/provision_client_repo.py \   --client profiles/clients/<client>.client.yml \   --client-repo C:/path/to/client-repo \   --docsops-dir docsops \   --install-scheduler windows
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: 3. Weekly automation (no manual commands)
records.content: Scheduler runs: - docsops/scripts/run_weekly_gap_batch.py It executes: - gap detection - stale checks - drift + docs contract (if enabled) - KPI/SLA (if enabled) - API-first flow (if enabled) - RAG/knowledge tasks:   - extract_knowledge_modules_from_docs.py   - validate_knowledge_modules.py   - generate_knowledge_retrieval_index.py - custom_tasks.weekly commands - consolidated report generation Output: - reports/consolidated_report.json - related reports are regenerated to the same filenames each run (no manual cleanup required) - reports/docsops_status.json (quick freshness/status check for non-technical users) Client repo .gitignore recommendation: reports/docsops-weekly.log
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: 4. Human role
records.content: \11. In file explorer, check Modified date of reports/consolidated_report.json. \11. If date/time is fresh, ask local LLM to process the report. \11. Review generated docs quickly. \11. Publish/merge.
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: 5. Operator manual checks after setup
records.content: \11. Check <client-repo>/docsops/config/client_runtime.yml for correct client values. \11. Check <client-repo>/docsops/policy_packs/selected.yml for expected policy pack/overrides. \11. Check <client-repo>/docsops/ENV_CHECKLIST.md and align secrets with client. \11. Run one smoke weekly cycle and ensure reports/consolidated_report.json is refreshed.
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: 6. Plan packaging
records.content: - Basic: essential quality + gaps + stale. - Pro: adds drift/contract, KPI/SLA, RAG/knowledge, hybrid/API-first. - Enterprise: strict policy, full automation surface, advanced verification. Details: docs/operations/PLAN_TIERS.md.
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: 7. What to say in sales calls
records.content: \11. "You get one-time setup, then weekly documentation ops on autopilot." \11. "Your team stops doing doc plumbing and only reviews final output." \11. "Quality is controlled by policy packs and automated gates." \11. "RAG/knowledge is maintained automatically, so AI outputs stay grounded."
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

hierarchy.lvl2: 7. What to say in sales calls

records.title: Canonical Flow (Sales + Delivery)
records.heading: 8. Compatibility mode
records.content: If needed, run equivalent weekly flow via GitHub Actions cron (weekly-consolidation.yml and companion workflows). Recommended mode remains local scheduler automation in client repo.
records.description: Canonical sales and delivery flow for onboarding and operating client Auto-Doc Pipeline setups.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.content: <!-- cspell:disable -->
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Централизованная настройка клиентов (очень просто)
records.content: Это схема, где вы настраиваете все в одном месте, а клиент работает локально как раньше.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Главное правило
records.content: - Вы запускаете scripts/onboard_client.py в своем мастер-репо. - Скрипт создает/обновляет profiles/clients/generated/<client_id>.client.yml. - Затем он собирает и ставит bundle в локальный клон клиентского репо. - Клиент дальше просто работает локально через Claude Code / Codex.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Что именно вы настраиваете (и где)
records.content: Один файл клиента: profiles/clients/<client>.client.yml. Полный справочник: - docs/operations/UNIFIED_CLIENT_CONFIG.md - docs/operations/PLAN_TIERS.md (готовые планы Basic/Pro/Enterprise) - docs/operations/PIPELINE_CAPABILITIES_CATALOG.md (полный список доступных команд/плюшек)
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: 1) Пути в конкретном репо клиента
records.content: runtime:   docs_root: "docs"   api_root: "openapi"   sdk_root: "clients" Если у клиента другой layout, меняете только эти 3 строки.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: 2) Какие генераторы документации нужны
records.content: runtime:   output_targets: ["sphinx", "readme", "github"] Примеры: - только Sphinx: ["sphinx"] - только ReadMe: ["readme"] - смешанный вариант: ["sphinx", "readme"]
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: 2.1) Как выбрать flow: code-first / api-first / hybrid
records.content: runtime:   docs_flow:     mode: "hybrid" - code-first: стандартный docs-ops поток - api-first: API-first поток - hybrid: оба потока одновременно
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

hierarchy.lvl3: 2.1) Как выбрать flow: code-first / api-first / hybrid

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: 3) Какие функции включены
records.content: Есть 2 уровня переключения: \11. Логические флаги для LLM: runtime:   modules:     gap_detection: true     drift_detection: true     docs_contract: true     kpi_sla: true     rag_optimization: true     lifecycle_management: true \11. Практический набор скриптов в bundle: bundle:   include_scripts:     - "scripts/check_docs_contract.py"     - "scripts/check_api_sdk_drift.py" Если функция не нужна, убираете флаг и соответствующий скрипт. По умолчанию baseline включен: - self-checks - fact/style checks - lifecycle checks/reports - SEO/GEO - RAG/knowledge index - drift/contract/KPI-SLA Ключевые возможности (ваше основное УТП) включаются через: runtime:   custom_tasks:     weekly:       - id: "openapi-lint"         enabled: true         command: "npm run lint:openapi"         continue_on_error: true Это не редкие спецзадачи, а базовый контур docs-ops платформы. В том числе: - SEO/GEO (seo_geo_optimizer.py) - RAG/knowledge index (generate_knowledge_retrieval_index.py) - интент-бандлы (build_
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: 3.1) Централизованные интеграции (Algolia + Ask AI)
records.content: runtime:   integrations:     algolia:       enabled: true       upload_on_weekly: true       app_id_env: "ALGOLIA_APP_ID"       api_key_env: "ALGOLIA_API_KEY"       index_name_env: "ALGOLIA_INDEX_NAME"     ask_ai:       enabled: true       auto_configure_on_provision: true       install_runtime_pack: false       provider: "openai"       billing_mode: "user-subscription"       model: "gpt-4.1-mini" Что это дает: - Algolia работает одинаково для всех поддерживаемых site generators. - Ask AI настраивается автоматически во время provisioning (без ручных CLI-команд).
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: 4) Насколько строгие правила качества
records.content: bundle:   base_policy_pack: "multi-product"   policy_overrides:     kpi_sla:       min_doc_coverage: 85 - base_policy_pack = базовый режим - policy_overrides = тонкая докрутка под клиента
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: 4.1) Как выбрать style guide (Vale)
records.content: bundle:   style_guide: "google" # google | microsoft | hybrid Сборщик сам положит .vale.ini в bundle под выбранный профиль.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: 5) Автовставка блока в AGENTS/CLAUDE
records.content: bundle:   llm:     codex_instructions_source: "AGENTS.md"     claude_instructions_source: "CLAUDE.md"     inject_managed_block: true     docsops_root_in_client_repo: "docsops" Сборщик автоматически положит в bundle: - AGENTS.md - CLAUDE.md И добавит в оба файла управляемый блок с инструкцией читать: - docsops/config/client_runtime.yml - docsops/policy_packs/selected.yml
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Команда сборки
records.content: cd "C:/Users/Kroha/Documents/development/Auto-Doc Pipeline" python3 scripts/build_client_bundle.py --client profiles/clients/blockstream-demo.client.yml Результат: generated/client_bundles/blockstream-demo/ Быстрый старт по пресетам: - profiles/clients/presets/small.yml - profiles/clients/presets/startup.yml - profiles/clients/presets/enterprise.yml Самый быстрый запуск: python3 scripts/onboard_client.py Скрипт задает вопросы, создает клиентский профиль, собирает bundle, ставит в клиентский репозиторий и устанавливает scheduler. onboard_client.py остается в вашем мастер-репо и не включается в клиентский bundle по умолчанию. Перед установкой вы видите preview профиля и подтверждаете запуск.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Команда "под ключ" (без возни клиента)
records.content: Если вы хотите сразу настроить и запускать за клиента, используйте provisioning: python3 scripts/provision_client_repo.py \   --client profiles/clients/blockstream-demo.client.yml \   --client-repo /path/to/client-repo \   --docsops-dir docsops \   --install-scheduler linux Для Windows: python3 scripts/provision_client_repo.py \   --client profiles/clients/blockstream-demo.client.yml \   --client-repo C:/path/to/client-repo \   --docsops-dir docsops \   --install-scheduler windows Что делает команда: \11. Собирает bundle из клиентского профиля. \11. Копирует bundle в репозиторий клиента (docsops/). \11. Генерирует docsops/ENV_CHECKLIST.md автоматически. \11. Ставит weekly scheduler (cron или Task Scheduler). \11. Клиенту не нужно руками настраивать pipeline.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Ручная проверка оператора (после установки)
records.content: \11. Проверить <client-repo>/docsops/config/client_runtime.yml. \11. Проверить <client-repo>/docsops/policy_packs/selected.yml. \11. Проверить <client-repo>/docsops/ENV_CHECKLIST.md и передать список env/secrets клиенту. \11. Убедиться, что scheduler установлен (cron entry или Task Scheduler task). \11. Запустить один тестовый weekly run и убедиться, что reports/consolidated_report.json обновился.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Как это ставится клиенту
records.content: Клиент кладет папку bundle в свой репо, например: <client-repo>/docsops/ Там уже будут: - config/client_runtime.yml - policy_packs/selected.yml - scripts/... - AGENTS.md - CLAUDE.md - LICENSE-COMMERCIAL.md - NOTICE - ops/run_weekly_docsops.sh / ops/run_weekly_docsops.ps1 - ops/install_cron_weekly.sh / ops/install_windows_task.ps1 - ops/runbook.md
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Не ломает ли это локальный опыт?
records.content: Не ломает. - Клиент по-прежнему работает локально. - LLM читает локальные AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md. - Все проверки и генерация выполняются локально в репо клиента. - Еженедельный отчёт формируется по расписанию автоматически.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

hierarchy.lvl2: Не ломает ли это локальный опыт?

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Еженедельный автоматический поток
records.content: Скрипт docsops/scripts/run_weekly_gap_batch.py по расписанию: \11. Собирает gap report за последние 7 дней. \11. Проверяет stale-доки (по умолчанию 180 дней без изменений). \11. Запускает KPI/SLA (если включено в bundle). \11. При mode=api-first|hybrid запускает API-first flow (если включено в runtime.api_first.enabled). \11. Запускает runtime.custom_tasks.weekly (если включены). \11. Создает reports/consolidated_report.json. Важно: API-first здесь только один из подпроцессов. Главный контур охватывает все типы документации и quality automation целиком. Порог stale можно менять для каждого клиента: - private_tuning.weekly_stale_days в profiles/clients/<client>.client.yml Дальше оператор или команда: \11. Передает consolidated report локальной LLM для batch generation. \11. Делает быстрый визуальный финальный просмотр готовых docs.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: Что добавить в `.gitignore` клиента
records.content: Рекомендуемый минимум: reports/docsops-weekly.log Это локальный лог планировщика. Его обычно не коммитят. JSON/Markdown отчеты (reports/consolidated_report.json и связанные отчеты) обычно оставляют в репозитории, если команда ведет историю отчетов в git.
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.title: Централизованная настройка клиентов
records.heading: RAG/knowledge без ручных запусков
records.content: Это уже укладывается в текущий smooth flow: \11. Один раз настроить профиль клиента (*.client.yml): - runtime.modules.knowledge_validation: true - runtime.modules.rag_optimization: true - runtime.custom_tasks.weekly для RAG-задач (например intent-сборки) - bundle.include_paths: ["knowledge_modules"] \11. Один раз сделать provisioning: - provision_client_repo.py ставит docsops/ в клиентский репозиторий - сразу ставится расписание (cron или Task Scheduler) \11. Далее weekly job идет автоматически: - запускается run_weekly_gap_batch.py - внутри выполняются:   - extract_knowledge_modules_from_docs.py   - validate_knowledge_modules.py   - generate_knowledge_retrieval_index.py   - любые custom_tasks.weekly (например build_all_intent_experiences.py) - отчеты формируются автоматически Итог: - клиентская команда не запускает команды вручную - человек только смотрит weekly report и выборочно проверяет финальные docs Поведение отчетов: - weekly запуск пишет в те же имена файлов - новые отчеты зам
records.description: Практический путь настройки клиентских bundle и автозапуска pipeline из одного места.

records.content: This document defines how pipeline functionality is packaged by client plan. Use this with: - profiles/clients/<client>.client.yml - docs/operations/UNIFIED_CLIENT_CONFIG.md
records.description: Feature packaging matrix and defaults for Basic, Pro, and Enterprise client plans.

records.title: Plan Tiers (Basic / Pro / Enterprise)
records.heading: 1. Feature matrix
records.content: | Capability | Basic | Pro | Enterprise | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Core quality gates (normalize, snippets, smoke) | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Gap detection | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Weekly stale checks | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Drift + docs contract gates | No | Yes | Yes | | KPI/SLA reports | No | Yes | Yes | | API-first flow | No | Optional | Full | | RAG/knowledge validation/index | No | Yes | Yes | | i18n sync | No | Optional | Yes | | SEO/GEO optimization weekly | Optional | Yes | Yes | | Custom weekly task slots | 2 | 6 | Unlimited | | Strict policy profile | minimal | api-first/monorepo | multi-product/plg + overrides |

records.heading: 3. How to apply a plan for a client
records.content: \11. Copy profiles/clients/_template.client.yml to profiles/clients/<client>.client.yml. \11. Copy one preset from this page into the new profile. \11. Adjust paths (docs_root, api_root, sdk_root) and output targets. \11. Build/install: python3 scripts/provision_client_repo.py \   --client profiles/clients/<client>.client.yml \   --client-repo /path/to/client-repo \   --docsops-dir docsops \   --install-scheduler linux

records.title: Unified Client Configuration
records.heading: Unified Client Configuration
records.content: Single source of truth for per-client setup: - profiles/clients/<client>.client.yml This file controls all client-specific behavior: repo paths, flow mode, modules, quality strictness, automation schedule, and legal labeling. Operator-first setup path (recommended): \11. Run python3 scripts/onboard_client.py. \11. Answer wizard questions (preset + client data + repo path + scheduler). \11. Review generated profile in profiles/clients/generated/<client_id>.client.yml. \11. Confirm install. \11. Verify outputs: - <client-repo>/docsops/config/client_runtime.yml - <client-repo>/docsops/policy_packs/selected.yml - <client-repo>/docsops/ENV_CHECKLIST.md Plan packaging reference: - docs/operations/PLAN_TIERS.md (Basic / Pro / Enterprise presets)
records.description: Single source of truth for per-client Auto-Doc Pipeline configuration, modules, and automation.

records.title: Unified Client Configuration
records.heading: Module -> required script in bundle
records.content: - gap_detection -> scripts/gap_detector.py - drift_detection -> scripts/check_api_sdk_drift.py - docs_contract -> scripts/check_docs_contract.py - kpi_sla -> scripts/evaluate_kpi_sla.py + scripts/generate_kpi_wall.py - normalization -> scripts/normalize_docs.py - snippet_lint -> scripts/lint_code_snippets.py - self_checks -> scripts/check_code_examples_smoke.py - fact_checks -> scripts/seo_geo_optimizer.py + scripts/doc_layers_validator.py - lifecycle_management -> scripts/lifecycle_manager.py (+ lifecycle report/redirect guidance) - knowledge_validation -> scripts/extract_knowledge_modules_from_docs.py + scripts/validate_knowledge_modules.py - rag_optimization -> scripts/generate_knowledge_retrieval_index.py - i18n_sync -> scripts/i18n_sync.py - release_pack -> scripts/generate_release_docs_pack.py - api-first/hybrid -> scripts/run_api_first_flow.py (+ related api-first scripts) If script is missing in bundle, module is skipped or warned. Important: API-first is only one flow branch.
records.description: Single source of truth for per-client Auto-Doc Pipeline configuration, modules, and automation.

records.title: Unified Client Configuration
records.heading: 9. Fully automated RAG/knowledge flow
records.content: To run RAG and knowledge base maintenance without manual commands: \11. Configure once in client profile: - enable runtime.modules.knowledge_validation: true - enable runtime.modules.rag_optimization: true - add runtime.custom_tasks.weekly entries (for example intent experiences) - include bundle.include_paths: ["knowledge_modules"] - include scripts:   - scripts/extract_knowledge_modules_from_docs.py   - scripts/validate_knowledge_modules.py   - scripts/generate_knowledge_retrieval_index.py \11. Provision once: - install bundle with scripts/provision_client_repo.py - install scheduler with --install-scheduler linux or --install-scheduler windows \11. Weekly automation then runs by itself: - scheduler triggers run_weekly_gap_batch.py - it runs extract_knowledge_modules_from_docs.py - it runs validate_knowledge_modules.py - it runs generate_knowledge_retrieval_index.py - it runs all enabled custom_tasks.weekly - it writes consolidated reports Outcome: - operators do not run commands man
records.description: Single source of truth for per-client Auto-Doc Pipeline configuration, modules, and automation.

records.title: Intent experience: automate for developer
records.heading: Define idempotent webhook retry handling
records.content: Provides retry and idempotency patterns to avoid duplicate processing across documentation, assistant guidance, and runbook automation. Use idempotency keys to make webhook retries safe. Persist a processed-event key for at least 24 hours, and skip duplicate events with HTTP 200 to stop upstream retries. Use exponential backoff for outbound retries: one second, two seconds, four seconds, eight seconds, and 16 seconds, capped at five attempts. const retryScheduleSeconds = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16]; function shouldProcess(eventId, cache) {   if (cache.has(eventId)) {     return false;   }   cache.add(eventId);   return true; } Alert when retry rate exceeds 5% for 15 minutes. This threshold usually indicates downstream instability.
records.description: Assembled guidance for one intent and audience using reusable knowledge modules with verified metadata and channel-ready sections.

records.title: Intent experience: configure for operator
records.heading: Configure HMAC authentication for inbound webhooks
records.content: Covers secure webhook authentication setup for docs, assistant responses, in-product hints, and automation workflows with one reusable module. Use HMAC validation to reject spoofed webhook requests before your workflow executes. Set the shared secret in {{ env_vars.webhook_url }} settings, then verify the X-Signature header with SHA-256. Reject requests older than 300 seconds, and return HTTP 401 for invalid signatures. curl -X POST "http://localhost:{{ default_webhook_port }}/webhook/order-events" \\   -H "Content-Type: application/json" \\   -H "X-Signature: sha256=YOUR_CALCULATED_SIGNATURE" \\   -d '{"order_id":"ord_9482","event":"order_paid","amount":129.99}' Keep replay protection enabled, rotate the secret every 90 days, and monitor 401 spikes for abuse detection.
records.description: Assembled guidance for one intent and audience using reusable knowledge modules with verified metadata and channel-ready sections.

records.title: Intent experience: secure for operator
records.heading: Configure HMAC authentication for inbound webhooks
records.content: Covers secure webhook authentication setup for docs, assistant responses, in-product hints, and automation workflows with one reusable module. Use HMAC validation to reject spoofed webhook requests before your workflow executes. Set the shared secret in {{ env_vars.webhook_url }} settings, then verify the X-Signature header with SHA-256. Reject requests older than 300 seconds, and return HTTP 401 for invalid signatures. curl -X POST "http://localhost:{{ default_webhook_port }}/webhook/order-events" \\   -H "Content-Type: application/json" \\   -H "X-Signature: sha256=YOUR_CALCULATED_SIGNATURE" \\   -d '{"order_id":"ord_9482","event":"order_paid","amount":129.99}' Keep replay protection enabled, rotate the secret every 90 days, and monitor 401 spikes for abuse detection.
records.description: Assembled guidance for one intent and audience using reusable knowledge modules with verified metadata and channel-ready sections.

records.title: Intent experience: troubleshoot for developer
records.heading: Define idempotent webhook retry handling
records.content: Provides retry and idempotency patterns to avoid duplicate processing across documentation, assistant guidance, and runbook automation. Use idempotency keys to make webhook retries safe. Persist a processed-event key for at least 24 hours, and skip duplicate events with HTTP 200 to stop upstream retries. Use exponential backoff for outbound retries: one second, two seconds, four seconds, eight seconds, and 16 seconds, capped at five attempts. const retryScheduleSeconds = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16]; function shouldProcess(eventId, cache) {   if (cache.has(eventId)) {     return false;   }   cache.add(eventId);   return true; } Alert when retry rate exceeds 5% for 15 minutes. This threshold usually indicates downstream instability.
records.description: Assembled guidance for one intent and audience using reusable knowledge modules with verified metadata and channel-ready sections.

records.title: Webhook node reference for
records.heading: Webhook node reference
records.content: The Webhook node is a trigger node that starts a workflow when it receives an HTTP request at a unique URL. It supports GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, and HEAD methods.
records.description: Complete parameter reference for the Webhook trigger node including HTTP methods, authentication, response modes, and binary data handling.

records.title: Webhook node reference for
records.heading: Parameters
records.content: | Parameter | Type | Default | Description | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | HTTP Method | enum | GET | HTTP method the webhook responds to. Options: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD | | Path | string | auto-generated UUID | URL path segment. The full URL is {base_url}/webhook/{path} | | Authentication | enum | None | Authentication method. Options: None, Basic Auth, Header Auth | | Respond | enum | When Last Node Finishes | When to send the HTTP response. Options: Immediately, When Last Node Finishes, Using Respond to Webhook Node | | Response Code | number | 200 | HTTP status code returned to the caller | | Response Data | enum | First Entry JSON | What data to return. Options: All Entries, First Entry JSON, First Entry Binary, No Response Body |
records.description: Complete parameter reference for the Webhook trigger node including HTTP methods, authentication, response modes, and binary data handling.

records.title: Webhook node reference for
records.heading: Authentication options
records.content: | Method | Credential type | Header checked | | --- | --- | --- | | None | — | — | | Basic Auth | Basic Auth | Authorization: Basic {base64} | | Header Auth | Header Auth | Custom header name/value pair |
records.description: Complete parameter reference for the Webhook trigger node including HTTP methods, authentication, response modes, and binary data handling.

records.title: Webhook node reference for
records.heading: URLs
records.content: Each Webhook node generates two URLs: - Test URL: Active only while the workflow editor is open and listening. Format: {base_url}/webhook-test/{path} - Production URL: Active when the workflow is toggled to Active. Format: {base_url}/webhook/{path} === "Cloud"  Base URL: <https://your-instance.app.the> product.cloud === "Self-hosted"  Base URL: your configured WEBHOOK_URL environment variable, or <http://localhost:5678> by default.
records.description: Complete parameter reference for the Webhook trigger node including HTTP methods, authentication, response modes, and binary data handling.

records.title: Webhook node reference for
records.heading: Output
records.content: The Webhook node outputs a single item with the following structure: {  "json": {  "headers": { "content-type": "application/json", "...": "..." },  "params": {},  "query": { "key": "value" },  "body": { "...": "request body..." }  } } For binary data (file uploads), the node outputs an additional binary key.
records.description: Complete parameter reference for the Webhook trigger node including HTTP methods, authentication, response modes, and binary data handling.

records.title: Webhook node reference for
records.heading: Smoke-checked examples
records.content: Use these minimal examples to verify that basic snippets still run in CI. python3 -c "print('webhook smoke ok')" payload = {"event": "ping", "status": "ok"} assert payload["status"] == "ok" print("webhook smoke ok")
records.description: Complete parameter reference for the Webhook trigger node including HTTP methods, authentication, response modes, and binary data handling.

records.title: Webhook node reference for
records.heading: Environment variables
records.content: | Variable | Default | Description | |----------|---------|-------------| | WEBHOOK_URL | <http://localhost:5678> | Base URL for webhook endpoints | | APP_PAYLOAD_SIZE_MAX | 16 | Maximum request body size in MB |
records.description: Complete parameter reference for the Webhook trigger node including HTTP methods, authentication, response modes, and binary data handling.

records.title: Troubleshooting Guide
records.heading: Troubleshooting
records.content: Problem → cause → solution. Start here when something breaks.
records.description: Solutions for common problems including webhook failures, credential errors, execution timeouts, and memory issues.

records.title: Fix: Webhook trigger not firing
records.heading: Fix: Webhook trigger not firing
records.content: The Webhook node does not respond to incoming HTTP requests. The sender receives a timeout, connection refused, or 404 error.
records.description: Troubleshoot Webhook nodes that do not receive requests. Common causes include inactive workflows, wrong URL type, and network configuration.

records.title: Fix: Webhook trigger not firing
records.heading: Cause 1: Using Test URL with an inactive editor
records.content: Symptom: Requests to the Test URL return a timeout or 404 after you close the editor tab. Why: The Test URL (/webhook-test/<id>) is only active while the workflow editor is open and the workflow is in Listening mode. Closing the browser tab deactivates it. Fix: Toggle the workflow to Active and use the Production URL (/webhook/<id>) instead. The Production URL remains active as long as the workflow is enabled.
records.description: Troubleshoot Webhook nodes that do not receive requests. Common causes include inactive workflows, wrong URL type, and network configuration.

hierarchy.lvl2: Cause 1: Using Test URL with an inactive editor

records.title: Fix: Webhook trigger not firing
records.heading: Cause 2: Workflow not activated
records.content: Symptom: Requests to the Production URL return 404 Not Found. Why: The Production URL only works when the workflow toggle is set to Active (green). An inactive workflow does not register its webhook endpoints. Fix: Open the workflow in the editor. Toggle the Active switch in the top-right corner. Verify the Production URL returns a response.
records.description: Troubleshoot Webhook nodes that do not receive requests. Common causes include inactive workflows, wrong URL type, and network configuration.

records.title: Fix: Webhook trigger not firing
records.heading: Cause 3: Firewall or reverse proxy blocking requests
records.content: Symptom: Requests from external services timeout. Local curl requests work fine. Why: The service listens on port 5678 by default. If your server firewall or reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy, Traefik) does not route traffic to this port, external requests never reach it. Fix: === "Docker with Nginx"  Verify your Nginx config routes traffic to the container:  ``nginx  location /webhook/ {  proxy_pass http://product:5678;  proxy_set_header Host $host;  proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;  } === "Direct install"  Check that port 5678 is open:  ``bash  sudo ufw allow 5678  # or  sudo firewall-cmd --add-port=5678/tcp --permanent
records.description: Troubleshoot Webhook nodes that do not receive requests. Common causes include inactive workflows, wrong URL type, and network configuration.

hierarchy.lvl2: Cause 3: Firewall or reverse proxy blocking requests

code_snippets: ```nginx  location /webhook/ {  proxy_pass http://product:5678;  proxy_set_header Host $host;  proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;  } === "Direct install"  Check that port 5678 is open:  ```

records.title: Fix: Webhook trigger not firing
records.heading: Cause 4: Incorrect WEBHOOK_URL environment variable
records.content: Symptom: The Webhook node shows a URL that does not match your domain. External services cannot reach it. Why: The service uses the WEBHOOK_URL environment variable to generate webhook URLs. If this is set to http://localhost:5678 (the default), external services cannot resolve localhost. Fix: Set WEBHOOK_URL to your public-facing URL: export WEBHOOK_URL=https://product.yourdomain.com
records.description: Troubleshoot Webhook nodes that do not receive requests. Common causes include inactive workflows, wrong URL type, and network configuration.

records.title: Fix: Webhook trigger not firing
records.heading: Still not working?
records.content: 1. Check the logs for errors: docker logs or the process output. 1. Test with a minimal curl command from the same network as the service. 1. Verify the HTTP method matches (the Webhook node only responds to the configured method).
records.description: Troubleshoot Webhook nodes that do not receive requests. Common causes include inactive workflows, wrong URL type, and network configuration.
